title: FluidNC-compatible board description
description: Describes the aspects of a FluidNC-compatible board that matter for writing a config.yaml for it: how MCU pins map to labeled connectors/functions, using the board's OWN label for a connector/pin (silkscreen or vendor documentation term), never an assumed FluidNC role. For example, a connector labeled 'X-' on the board is documented as exactly that -- a user may legitimately wire it to any axis's limit switch, including Z.  Companion to mcu-chip.schema.json (the lower layer: silicon/module-level GPIO facts, referenced here via board.mcu_chip) and, eventually, to fluidnc-config-schema.json in the sibling FluidNC repo (cross-checking that a generated config.yaml only uses pins this board actually exposes is a planned later phase, not implemented by this schema alone).  Design decisions worth knowing when reading this schema: - No pin-uniqueness constraint at this level. A board may legitimately offer the   same physical MCU pin for two different purposes (e.g. a GPIO appearing at both   a laser-TTL terminal and a spindle terminal) -- uniqueness only becomes a real   constraint once a specific config.yaml binds pins to FluidNC functions. - No inheritance between board variants/revisions. Each board, including each   member of a casually-named 'family' of hardware revisions, is a complete,   independent file. `family` is purely an informational grouping label. - Motor driver pins are grouped by physical socket, never split apart. A socket's   `driver_class` determines which pins it has and whether its ultimate FluidNC   motor type is fixed (onboard_fixed) or up to the config author to choose   (external_connector, stepstick_socket).

rs485_channels.description: RS485 buses this board exposes as a coupled tx_pin/rx_pin(/rts_pin) resource, distinct from both fixed_peripherals (which are never reassignable) and plain generic_pins (single, uncoupled pins) -- an RS485 channel is a board resource in its own right that a config author can bind to a FluidNC use, most commonly a Modbus VFD spindle (FluidNC's VFD spindle type, backed by a uartN: block using this channel's pins), though the board fact recorded here is just the bus's existence and pins, not a binding to that or any other specific FluidNC function.

expansion_uarts.description: Dedicated UART-based expansion connectors (e.g. an RJ12 jack for pendants/I-O-expansion modules like FluidDial/Airedale) whose pins/baud/mode are fixed board facts, same general shape as fixed_peripherals.dedicated_uarts -- but UNLIKE dedicated_uarts (whose pins are entirely internal and never appear in a generated config.yaml, e.g. a bus soldered straight to onboard TMC2209 chips), an expansion_uart's pins ARE meant to be written into config.yaml, as a uartN: block plus a paired uart_channelN: block, because the far end is an external, config-author-visible device rather than an onboard chip. Which uart_num a config actually uses for this is a wizard-level/config-authoring decision (conventionally uart1, or uart2 if the board also has UART-addressed onboard TMC drivers already claiming uart1 -- see motor_socket.extra.bus), not a board fact recorded here.

i2c_connectors.description: A board's I2C-capable pin pair(s), brought out to a connector, NOT a fixed_peripheral. Unlike spi/i2so (which are always consumed by an onboard peripheral that requires them), FluidNC rarely has anything onboard actually using I2C -- its only I2C-consuming feature, an OLED display, is deprecated -- so these pins are almost always just available at a connector for the config author to use or not, same category of decision as an expansion_uart connector. I2C pins are also frequently bare GPIOs with no other fixed role, so the same connector is commonly repurposed entirely as a generic UART instead (scl_pin plays the tx_pin role, sda_pin plays the rx_pin role) rather than as an actual I2C bus. Recorded here as a bindable resource: a config author may leave it unused, bind it to a real i2cN: bus, or bind it as a generic expansion UART -- which of those (if any) is a config-authoring decision, not a board fact.

mcu_pin.description: Bare MCU-side pin reference: gpio.N (native GPIO) or i2so.N (I2S-expander virtual pin). No :high/:low/:pu/:pd/:dsN attributes here -- those are config-authoring/wiring choices, not board facts. Matches the pin-string vocabulary of fluidnc-config-schema.json minus its attribute suffixes.

id.description: Lowercase snake_case identifier. Filename should be <id>.board.yaml.

name.description: Human-readable board name, as it's commonly known.

mcu_chip.description: chip_id of the chip-facts file this board uses, e.g. 'esp32'.

family.description: Optional, INFORMATIONAL ONLY grouping label for boards that are casually known as variants/revisions of each other (e.g. several 'MKS DLC32' boards). Has no schema or data effect -- there is no inheritance between family members; each is a complete, independent file. Purely for search/display grouping.

revision.description: Optional hardware revision/version string, e.g. 'V2.1', for boards that need one to disambiguate within a family.

status.description: active: currently sold/supported. discontinued: no longer sold but historically valid. unverified: not yet cross-checked against a schematic/real hardware. community_documented: sourced from community reporting rather than official vendor documentation (e.g. MKS DLC32) -- treat with more caution than 'active'/'discontinued'.

meta.description: Non-technical reference information. Deliberately minimal for now; expected to grow.

engines.description: Ordered list of FluidNC stepping engines this board's motor pin wiring can use. First entry is preferred. A board whose motor pins are wired through I2SO (virtual pins, not real GPIOs -- see fixed_peripherals.i2so / motor socket pins using i2so.N) can ONLY use the i2s engine and will have a single-element list. A board using native GPIO throughout may list more than one, e.g. [rmt, timed], even though 'timed' is strictly worse -- the list should honestly reflect what would work, not just what's recommended. Note: I2S_STATIC and I2S_STREAM are functionally identical in FluidNC and are both represented by the single value 'i2s' here -- which specific engine string to write in a generated config.yaml is a config-authoring detail, not a board fact.

notes.description: Free-form caveats about engine choice on real hardware, e.g. a board-specific artifact with one otherwise-equivalent engine variant that isn't a general FluidNC behavior.

fixed_peripherals.description: Onboard peripherals whose pins are hardwired by the board design and are NOT subject to user reassignment.

notes.description: E.g. note here if the SD card shares its physical SPI bus with an onboard SPI motor driver, which can constrain usable clock frequency.

pin_count.description: How many i2so.N virtual pins this board's shift-register chain actually provides. Stated explicitly per-board rather than assumed -- real-world wiki documentation has been wrong about this before.

dedicated_uarts.description: UART buses whose physical pins are entirely hardwired to an onboard fixed driver chip (or other onboard peripheral) and are NOT available for reassignment -- e.g. a UART bus soldered directly to onboard TMC2209 chips. Distinct from a motor_socket's extra.bus field, which just names which logical uartN: a socket's config should reference; this records the actual fixed pin/baud/mode facts of that bus. A board with more than one such bus (rare) can list more than one entry.

label.description: What this bus is dedicated to, e.g. 'TMC2209 UART'.

baud.description: Fixed baud rate, if the onboard peripheral requires a specific one.

mode.description: Fixed UartData mode (e.g. '8N1'), if the onboard peripheral requires a specific one.

shared_disable_pin.description: Present only if ALL sockets in this file truly share one disable line (mirrors FluidNC's own axes.shared_stepper_disable_pin, which is a single global setting, not partial -- and matches FluidNC's own field name, disable_pin, not 'enable_pin'). If present, no individual socket may declare its own disable_pin. If absent, every socket must declare its own (even if some happen to coincide in practice).

shared_reset_pin.description: Same either/or rule as shared_disable_pin, for stepstick sockets with a ganged driver RESET line.

notes.description: Board-level note about the motor sockets as a group, e.g. a fixed total axis count.

label.description: The board's OWN label for this socket (e.g. 'X', 'Motor1', 'M3') -- purely positional/cosmetic, not a binding to that FluidNC axis. A user may assign this socket to any axis.

generic_name.description: Normalized, vendor-independent name for this connector/pin, used by tools (e.g. the mapping wizard) to present a consistent vocabulary across boards instead of each board's own silkscreen/vendor label. Algorithmic per a fixed naming scheme (e.g. plain digital inputs are "In<pin>", 5V push-pull outputs "5V Out<pin>", MOSFET low-side outputs "FET<pin>", open-collector outputs "OC Out<pin>", onboard-driven motor sockets "Motor<N>", external-driver headers "Driver<N>", spindle-control digital outputs "Spindle Out<pin>", the board's 10V analog spindle output "Spindle 10V"). "<pin>" is the mcu_pin number/identifier: a plain gpio.N pin is written as just N with no separator (e.g. "FET4" for gpio.4). An i2so.N pin (the Nth, 0-indexed, pin of the I2S shift-register chain) is written as I2SN, WITH a preceding space before it, unlike the bare-number gpio case (e.g. "OC Out I2S7", not "OC OutI2S7", for i2so.7 -- the space keeps the rendered label legible) -- needed for any board whose auxiliary outputs are wired through the I2S chain rather than a native GPIO, e.g. some 6-pack-family boards' extra I2SO-driven outputs. Optional -- omit (or set equal to label) for a connector/pin whose generic naming convention hasn't been pinned down yet; a tool should fall back to label in that case.

driver_class.description: external_connector: step/dir/enable go to a connector for an off-board driver via level translation -- FluidNC motor type is always standard_stepper, chosen by the config author, not knowable from the board alone. stepstick_socket: a socket for an interchangeable driver module (A4988/DRV8825/TMC2209-standalone/etc.) -- FluidNC motor type is presumptively stepstick, though a knowledgeable user could wire a fancier module differently; current is set by a trimpot on the module, not software. onboard_fixed: a specific driver chip is soldered on and cannot be swapped -- the FluidNC motor type IS a known board fact (see fixed_motor_type). IMPORTANT ASYMMETRY: stepstick_socket does NOT preclude using an external driver instead -- pin-compatible adapter boards exist that plug into a stepstick socket and break its step/dir/disable signals out to screw terminals/headers for an off-board driver, and some boards additionally build this in as a second, dedicated connector wired to the same socket signals. Either way, this is a general property of stepstick_socket itself, not something that needs recording per board. The reverse is NOT true: external_connector genuinely precludes stepstick use -- there is no socket to plug a module into. If a specific board has a literal, dedicated second connector for the same motor position (as opposed to the generic 'a third-party adapter could exist' case), record that in this socket's notes field for now; a more structured field can be added if/when enough real boards need one to justify it.

fixed_motor_type.description: Required if and only if driver_class is onboard_fixed. The exact fluidnc-config-schema.json motor type name this socket's chip corresponds to, e.g. 'tmc_2209', 'tmc_5160'.

extra.description: Driver-class-specific extra facts that aren't raw MCU pins -- e.g. {bus: uart1, addr: 0} for an onboard UART-addressed chip, or {spi_index: 1} for an SPI daisy chain position. Deliberately loosely typed for now; expected to firm up as more onboard_fixed boards are documented.

allOf.description: Non-stepper fixed actuators (currently just rc_servo) use a single output_pin instead of step/direction_pin. Extend this enum as more onboard_fixed non-stepper types are documented (e.g. solenoid, dynamixel2).

motor_socket_pins.description: Field-level requirements are NOT enforced here -- see motor_socket's allOf, which dispatches required fields based on driver_class/fixed_motor_type (a stepper-driven socket needs step_pin+direction_pin; a non-stepper fixed actuator like rc_servo needs output_pin instead).

disable_pin.description: Omit if this socket's disable line is covered by motor_sockets.shared_disable_pin instead.

reset_pin.description: stepstick_socket only. Omit if covered by motor_sockets.shared_reset_pin instead.

cs_pin.description: onboard_fixed with an SPI-driven chip only.

output_pin.description: onboard_fixed with a non-stepper fixed actuator only (fixed_motor_type: rc_servo/solenoid/etc.) -- a single PWM/signal pin instead of step/direction.

conditioning.description: raw: bare MCU GPIO with at most internal/board pull resistors, nothing else in the signal path. opto_isolated / rc_filtered / level_shifted / comparator: real signal-conditioning circuitry between the connector and the MCU pin.

external_pullup_required.description: True if the circuit expects an external pull resistor (e.g. for an open-collector/NPN sensor) that the board does not itself provide.

voltage_max.description: Maximum safe input voltage this circuit tolerates, if higher than plain 3.3V logic (relevant for opto-isolated/level-shifted inputs).

drive_type.description: totem_pole: standard push-pull logic output. open_collector: sinks only, needs an external pull-up. mosfet_low_side / mosfet_high_side: switches load to ground / to supply, respectively. relay: mechanical contact. h_bridge: bidirectional drive (e.g. HBridge spindle output).

voltage_max.description: Maximum voltage this output can switch/drive.

current_max_a.description: Maximum current this output can source/sink.

label.description: The board's OWN label for this connector/pin (silkscreen or vendor documentation term), e.g. 'X-', 'Coolant', 'Probe'. Not a binding to that FluidNC function -- purely positional/cosmetic.

generic_name.description: Normalized, vendor-independent name for this connector/pin, used by tools (e.g. the mapping wizard) to present a consistent vocabulary across boards instead of each board's own silkscreen/vendor label. Algorithmic per a fixed naming scheme (e.g. plain digital inputs are "In<pin>", 5V push-pull outputs "5V Out<pin>", MOSFET low-side outputs "FET<pin>", open-collector outputs "OC Out<pin>", onboard-driven motor sockets "Motor<N>", external-driver headers "Driver<N>", spindle-control digital outputs "Spindle Out<pin>", the board's 10V analog spindle output "Spindle 10V"). "<pin>" is the mcu_pin number/identifier: a plain gpio.N pin is written as just N with no separator (e.g. "FET4" for gpio.4). An i2so.N pin (the Nth, 0-indexed, pin of the I2S shift-register chain) is written as I2SN, WITH a preceding space before it, unlike the bare-number gpio case (e.g. "OC Out I2S7", not "OC OutI2S7", for i2so.7 -- the space keeps the rendered label legible) -- needed for any board whose auxiliary outputs are wired through the I2S chain rather than a native GPIO, e.g. some 6-pack-family boards' extra I2SO-driven outputs. Optional -- omit (or set equal to label) for a connector/pin whose generic naming convention hasn't been pinned down yet; a tool should fall back to label in that case.

direction.description: input/output: fixed, single-purpose pin. bidirectional: driven and read as a single shared signal, both directions active at once (e.g. a true I2C SDA line, or any other genuinely open-drain shared-wire signal) -- input_profile and output_profile together describe that one shared electrical path. either: a bare, uncommitted GPIO that a config author can choose to use as EITHER a plain input OR a plain output (never both at once) -- e.g. a repurposable pin broken out to a header with no fixed onboard role. Also requires both input_profile and output_profile, but here they describe two independent, mutually-exclusive uses rather than one shared signal; note this in the entry's notes if worth clarifying further.

label.description: The board's own label for this general-purpose expansion/module socket, distinct from a motor_socket -- e.g. a pluggable I/O module header.

generic_name.description: Normalized, vendor-independent name for this connector/pin, used by tools (e.g. the mapping wizard) to present a consistent vocabulary across boards instead of each board's own silkscreen/vendor label. Algorithmic per a fixed naming scheme (e.g. plain digital inputs are "In<pin>", 5V push-pull outputs "5V Out<pin>", MOSFET low-side outputs "FET<pin>", open-collector outputs "OC Out<pin>", onboard-driven motor sockets "Motor<N>", external-driver headers "Driver<N>", spindle-control digital outputs "Spindle Out<pin>", the board's 10V analog spindle output "Spindle 10V"). "<pin>" is the mcu_pin number/identifier: a plain gpio.N pin is written as just N with no separator (e.g. "FET4" for gpio.4). An i2so.N pin (the Nth, 0-indexed, pin of the I2S shift-register chain) is written as I2SN, WITH a preceding space before it, unlike the bare-number gpio case (e.g. "OC Out I2S7", not "OC OutI2S7", for i2so.7 -- the space keeps the rendered label legible) -- needed for any board whose auxiliary outputs are wired through the I2S chain rather than a native GPIO, e.g. some 6-pack-family boards' extra I2SO-driven outputs. Optional -- omit (or set equal to label) for a connector/pin whose generic naming convention hasn't been pinned down yet; a tool should fall back to label in that case.

pinout_class.description: Optional shared identifier for a common connector pinout used across multiple boards/modules (e.g. a subset-compatible family of module headers), purely informational.

module_pin.description: The connector's own pin identifier (number or label).

direction.description: See generic_pin.direction for the bidirectional vs. either distinction (shared single signal vs. a config-author's choice of one or the other).

label.description: The board's own label for this RS485/Modbus bus (e.g. 'RS485', 'Modbus'). Purely positional/cosmetic, not a binding to any particular FluidNC use.

generic_name.description: Normalized, vendor-independent name for this connector/pin, used by tools (e.g. the mapping wizard) to present a consistent vocabulary across boards instead of each board's own silkscreen/vendor label. Algorithmic per a fixed naming scheme (e.g. plain digital inputs are "In<pin>", 5V push-pull outputs "5V Out<pin>", MOSFET low-side outputs "FET<pin>", open-collector outputs "OC Out<pin>", onboard-driven motor sockets "Motor<N>", external-driver headers "Driver<N>", spindle-control digital outputs "Spindle Out<pin>", the board's 10V analog spindle output "Spindle 10V"). "<pin>" is the mcu_pin number/identifier: a plain gpio.N pin is written as just N with no separator (e.g. "FET4" for gpio.4). An i2so.N pin (the Nth, 0-indexed, pin of the I2S shift-register chain) is written as I2SN, WITH a preceding space before it, unlike the bare-number gpio case (e.g. "OC Out I2S7", not "OC OutI2S7", for i2so.7 -- the space keeps the rendered label legible) -- needed for any board whose auxiliary outputs are wired through the I2S chain rather than a native GPIO, e.g. some 6-pack-family boards' extra I2SO-driven outputs. Optional -- omit (or set equal to label) for a connector/pin whose generic naming convention hasn't been pinned down yet; a tool should fall back to label in that case.

rts_pin.description: Optional. Present only if this bus has a dedicated, MCU-driven direction/driver-enable pin for the RS485 transceiver. Many boards (e.g. those built around a MAX3485 wired for automatic direction sensing, or simple half-duplex TX/RX with no software direction control) have no separate RTS pin at all -- omit rather than guessing one.

pins_reusable_as_generic_io.description: True if, when this channel is not used for RS485, its tx_pin/rx_pin can individually be wired/used as plain digital I/O instead (as documented for some boards, where the same physical terminal doubles as a 5V logic output/input when the RS485 transceiver isn't in use). This is a fact about the physical circuit -- if true, the same mcu_pin(s) may also legitimately appear in this board's generic_pins, per this schema's general no-pin-uniqueness-at-this-level design (see top-level description).

notes.description: E.g. which transceiver chip is used (affects voltage/isolation characteristics and real-world direction-control behavior).

expansion_uart.description: No rts_pin/cts_pin fields -- confirmed against real vendor documentation (both the Corgi and 4x wiki pages' own example config for this exact connector) that this physical connector (a 2-wire-data RJ12/similar jack) never carries RTS/CTS lines at all; a generated config.yaml should write literal NO_PIN for both rather than this schema pretending they might sometimes be real pins, unlike rs485_channel's genuinely-sometimes-present rts_pin.

label.description: The board's own label for this connector, e.g. 'RJ12 Expansion'.

generic_name.description: Normalized, vendor-independent name for this connector/pin, used by tools (e.g. the mapping wizard) to present a consistent vocabulary across boards instead of each board's own silkscreen/vendor label. Algorithmic per a fixed naming scheme (e.g. plain digital inputs are "In<pin>", 5V push-pull outputs "5V Out<pin>", MOSFET low-side outputs "FET<pin>", open-collector outputs "OC Out<pin>", onboard-driven motor sockets "Motor<N>", external-driver headers "Driver<N>", spindle-control digital outputs "Spindle Out<pin>", the board's 10V analog spindle output "Spindle 10V"). "<pin>" is the mcu_pin number/identifier: a plain gpio.N pin is written as just N with no separator (e.g. "FET4" for gpio.4). An i2so.N pin (the Nth, 0-indexed, pin of the I2S shift-register chain) is written as I2SN, WITH a preceding space before it, unlike the bare-number gpio case (e.g. "OC Out I2S7", not "OC OutI2S7", for i2so.7 -- the space keeps the rendered label legible) -- needed for any board whose auxiliary outputs are wired through the I2S chain rather than a native GPIO, e.g. some 6-pack-family boards' extra I2SO-driven outputs. Optional -- omit (or set equal to label) for a connector/pin whose generic naming convention hasn't been pinned down yet; a tool should fall back to label in that case.

connector_type.description: Optional physical connector type, e.g. 'RJ12'. Purely informational.

baud.description: Fixed baud rate required by the expansion protocol (e.g. 1000000 for FluidNC's own pendant/expander protocol).

i2c_connector.description: A board's I2C-capable pin pair, brought out to a connector -- see the top-level i2c_connectors field for why this is bindable rather than a fixed_peripheral. No baud/mode/notes-required fields for the bus itself: unlike expansion_uart (whose far end is always the same fixed protocol), this connector's actual use (real I2C bus vs. repurposed generic UART, vs. left alone) is entirely a config-authoring decision.

label.description: The board's own label for this connector, e.g. 'I2C', 'OLED'.

generic_name.description: Normalized, vendor-independent name for this connector/pin, used by tools (e.g. the mapping wizard) to present a consistent vocabulary across boards instead of each board's own silkscreen/vendor label. Algorithmic per a fixed naming scheme (e.g. plain digital inputs are "In<pin>", 5V push-pull outputs "5V Out<pin>", MOSFET low-side outputs "FET<pin>", open-collector outputs "OC Out<pin>", onboard-driven motor sockets "Motor<N>", external-driver headers "Driver<N>", spindle-control digital outputs "Spindle Out<pin>", the board's 10V analog spindle output "Spindle 10V"). "<pin>" is the mcu_pin number/identifier: a plain gpio.N pin is written as just N with no separator (e.g. "FET4" for gpio.4). An i2so.N pin (the Nth, 0-indexed, pin of the I2S shift-register chain) is written as I2SN, WITH a preceding space before it, unlike the bare-number gpio case (e.g. "OC Out I2S7", not "OC OutI2S7", for i2so.7 -- the space keeps the rendered label legible) -- needed for any board whose auxiliary outputs are wired through the I2S chain rather than a native GPIO, e.g. some 6-pack-family boards' extra I2SO-driven outputs. Optional -- omit (or set equal to label) for a connector/pin whose generic naming convention hasn't been pinned down yet; a tool should fall back to label in that case.
